case.question: Which 2026 light rail transit line in Toronto, Ontario opened on February 8, 2026 with 25 stations along Eglinton Avenue, and uses standard gauge instead of the broad gauge used by the city's older heavy rail subway lines?
case.expected_behavior: Line 5 Eglinton (Eglinton Crosstown LRT / 'the Crosstown'). Operated by the Toronto Transit Commission, opened February 8, 2026, 19 km long, 25 stations, standard gauge.

agent_run.question: Which 2026 light rail transit line in Toronto, Ontario opened on February 8, 2026 with 25 stations along Eglinton Avenue, and uses standard gauge instead of the broad gauge used by the city's older heavy rail subway lines?
agent_run.answer:
The answer is clearly confirmed by Wikipedia. The line you're describing is **Line 5 Eglinton**, also known as the **Eglinton Crosstown LRT** (or simply "the Crosstown") [Line 5 Eglinton].

Here are the key details, all verified:

- **Location:** Toronto, Ontario, Canada, running along Eglinton Avenue [Line 5 Eglinton]
- **Opening date:** Service began on **February 8, 2026**, as part of a phased opening [Line 5 Eglinton]
- **Stations:** The line has **25 stations** and is 19 kilometres long [Line 5 Eglinton]
- **Track gauge:** It uses **standard gauge**, rather than the broad "Toronto gauge" used by the city's three older heavy rail subway lines (Lines 1, 2, and 4) [Line 5 Eglinton]
- **Type:** It is a **light rail transit (LRT)** line, operated by the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) as part of the Toronto subway system [Line 5 Eglinton]

The line was originally conceived in 2007 under Mayor David Miller as part of the Transit City plan, and after years of delays and legal disputes with the construction consortium Crosslinx, it finally opened to the public in early 2026 [Line 5 Eglinton].

Sources:
- Line 5 Eglinton

case.question: Which Birmingham railway line, used primarily by freight trains since the 1940s, reopened on April 7, 2026 to local passenger services after a break of over 80 years, with three new/reopened stations named Moseley Village, Kings Heath, and Pineapple Road?
case.expected_behavior: Camp Hill line. Local passenger services ended in 1941 (confirmed permanently closed 1946); reopened to passengers on April 7, 2026 after ~84 years. Three reopened/new stations: Moseley Village, Kings Heath, Pineapple Road. ELR designation: St Andrews Junction to Kings Norton line.

agent_run.question: Which Birmingham railway line, used primarily by freight trains since the 1940s, reopened on April 7, 2026 to local passenger services after a break of over 80 years, with three new/reopened stations named Moseley Village, Kings Heath, and Pineapple Road?
agent_run.answer:
The line you're referring to is the **Camp Hill line**.

The Camp Hill line is a railway line in Birmingham running between Kings Norton and Birmingham New Street [Camp Hill line]. Local passenger services on the line ended in the 1940s — specifically, all six passenger stations were closed in 1941 as a wartime rationalisation of the network, and the closures were confirmed as permanent in 1946 [Camp Hill line]. From then until 2026, the line was used primarily by **freight trains** and some longer-distance passenger trains only [Camp Hill line].

After a break of over 80 years (nearly 84 years), the line and its stations officially **reopened on 7 April 2026**, with three new/reopened stations named **Moseley Village**, **Kings Heath**, and **Pineapple Road** [Camp Hill line].

Sources:
- Camp Hill line

The stations you mention — Moseley Village, Kings Heath, and Pineapple Road — were indeed the proposed new stations associated with that reopening.
results.reference_answer: The Camp Hill line in Birmingham reopened to local passenger services on 7 April 2026, after local services had ended in the 1940s (with stations closed in 1941 and permanently confirmed closed in 1946). From then until 2026, the line was used primarily by freight trains and some longer-distance passenger trains. The three reopened stations are named Moseley Village, Kings Heath, and Pineapple Road [Camp Hill line].

case.question: Which 2026 tramway line in Switzerland is the country's first tramway built using standard gauge (rather than the metric gauge used by other Swiss tramways), uses Stadler Tramlink trams manufactured in Valencia, Spain, and marks the return of streetcars to its city after the previous tram system closed in 1964?
case.expected_behavior: Lausanne tramway, Line T1 — opens late 2026 (Phase 1, 4.6 km, between Lausanne-Flon and Renens stations, 10 stations). First Swiss tramway with standard gauge. Fleet of 10 Stadler Tramlink trams, constructed in Valencia, Spain. The previous Lausanne tram network closed in 1964 (60+ years prior).

agent_run.question: Which 2026 tramway line in Switzerland is the country's first tramway built using standard gauge (rather than the metric gauge used by other Swiss tramways), uses Stadler Tramlink trams manufactured in Valencia, Spain, and marks the return of streetcars to its city after the previous tram system closed in 1964?
agent_run.answer:
All three criteria are confirmed in the Wikipedia article. Here is the full answer:

---

The tramway line you're describing is **Line T1 of the Lausanne tramway** in Lausanne, Switzerland [Lausanne tramway].

Here's how it ticks every box:

- **Standard gauge first:** Line T1 is explicitly described as "the first tramline in Switzerland to use standard gauge tracks, the others using metric gauge." [Lausanne tramway]
- **Stadler Tramlink trams from Valencia:** The line will use 10 **Stadler Tramlink** trams — seven-section, low-floor vehicles 45 metres long with a capacity of 316 passengers — which were constructed in **Valencia, Spain**. [Lausanne tramway]
- **Return of trams after 1964 closure:** Its opening in **late 2026** will mark the return of trams (streetcars) to Lausanne after the city's former tram system **closed in 1964**, over 60 years prior. [Lausanne tramway]

Phase 1 of Line T1 runs 4.6 km between **Lausanne-Flon** (city centre) and **Renens** railway station, with a Phase 2 extension to Croix-Péage in Villars-Sainte-Croix expected in 2027.

Sources:
- Lausanne tramway

case.question: Which 2026 monorail line in São Paulo, Brazil — originally one of the transportation projects supporting a 2014 international football tournament — opened in 2026 after delays of more than a decade, has 14 stations including a branch to a major airport, and is the first line in its metro system to feature a branch?
case.expected_behavior: Line 17 (Gold) / Linha 17–Ouro of the São Paulo Metro. Originally a 2014 FIFA World Cup transportation project. 14 stations from São Paulo-Morumbi to Jabaquara, with a branch to Congonhas station (São Paulo/Congonhas Airport). First SP Metro line to have a branch. Pushed from 2013 original opening to June 2026.

agent_run.question: Which 2026 monorail line in São Paulo, Brazil — originally one of the transportation projects supporting a 2014 international football tournament — opened in 2026 after delays of more than a decade, has 14 stations including a branch to a major airport, and is the first line in its metro system to feature a branch?
agent_run.answer:
The answer is clear and well-supported. Here it is:

---

The line you're describing is **Line 17 – Gold** (*Linha 17–Ouro*) of the **São Paulo Metro** [Line 17 (São Paulo Metro)].

Here are the key confirmed details:

- **Type:** Monorail [Line 17 (São Paulo Metro)]
- **Stations:** 14 stations in total, beginning at **São Paulo-Morumbi** and ending at **Jabaquara**, with a **branch serving Congonhas Airport** (São Paulo/Congonhas Airport) [Line 17 (São Paulo Metro)]
- **First in the system with a branch:** It is the first line in the São Paulo Metro system to feature a branch, forming a distinctive "Y" shape [Line 17 (São Paulo Metro)]
- **2014 FIFA World Cup origins:** The line was originally one of the transportation projects supporting the **2014 FIFA World Cup**, with the first phase initially planned to open in the first half of **2014** [Line 17 (São Paulo Metro)]
- **Decades of delays:** Construction was plagued by environmental approval issues, a fatal beam collapse in June 2014, contract disputes (including the termination of the original consortium after Scomi's financial trouble), and repeated postponements — pushing the completion date from 2013 all the way to **2026** [Line 17 (São Paulo Metro)]

Sources:

case.question: Which suburban rapid transit line in mainland China, opened on April 22, 2026, became the first inter-provincial subway in its host city and the second in mainland China overall (after Shanghai Metro Line 11 connecting Shanghai and Suzhou)? Tie-breaker: the first subway line in mainland China to be jointly constructed, managed, and operated by two provinces.
case.expected_behavior: Line S2 of the Nanjing Metro. Officially opened at 10:00 on April 22, 2026. First inter-provincial subway in Nanjing; second in mainland China after Shanghai Metro Line 11 (Shanghai–Suzhou). First in mainland China jointly constructed/managed/operated by two provinces (Jiangsu and Anhui). Connects Nanjing and Ma'anshan.

agent_run.question: Which suburban rapid transit line in mainland China, opened on April 22, 2026, became the first inter-provincial subway in its host city and the second in mainland China overall (after Shanghai Metro Line 11 connecting Shanghai and Suzhou)? Tie-breaker: the first subway line in mainland China to be jointly constructed, managed, and operated by two provinces.
agent_run.answer:
The answer is confirmed in full detail. Here it is:

---

The line in question is **Nanjing Metro Line S2** (南京地铁S2号线), a suburban rapid transit line in the **Nanjing Metro** system [Line S2 (Nanjing Metro)].

Key facts, all verified from Wikipedia:

- **Opened:** 22 April 2026, at 10:00 [Line S2 (Nanjing Metro)]
- **Inter-provincial significance:** It is the **first inter-provincial subway in Nanjing** and the **second in Mainland China overall**, after Shanghai Metro Line 11, which connects Shanghai and Suzhou (Jiangsu province) [Line S2 (Nanjing Metro)]
- **Tie-breaker distinction:** It is also the **first subway line in Mainland China to be jointly constructed, managed, and operated by two provinces** — namely Jiangsu province (Nanjing) and Anhui province (Ma'anshan) [Line S2 (Nanjing Metro)]
- The line's construction was rooted in a 2020 co-construction cooperation agreement signed by the mayors of Nanjing and Ma'anshan, with the Ma'anshan section beginning construction on 30 December 2020 and the Nanjing section on 28 December 2021 [Line S2 (Nanjing Metro)]
- Express trains complete the full journey across the line in just 30 minutes [Line S2 (Nanjing Metro)]

case.question: Which 2026 people-mover line, approximately 2.6 km (1.6 mi) long with 4 stations, connects a São Paulo international airport to a nearby CPTM railway station, and opened to airport employees on December 3, 2025 before opening to the public on February 20, 2026?

agent_run.answer:
The answer is the **GRU Airport People Mover** [GRU Airport People Mover].

This is a people-mover line at the **São Paulo/Guarulhos International Airport** that connects the airport's terminals to the nearby **Aeroporto-Guarulhos station** on CPTM's **Line 13-Jade** [GRU Airport People Mover]. The line is approximately **2.6 km (1.6 mi) long** and has **4 stations** — one at the CPTM railway station and one at each of the three airport terminals [GRU Airport People Mover].

The system uses **Aeromovel** technology, installed by Brazilian firm Aerom (the same company behind the Metro-Airport Connection at Porto Alegre's Salgado Filho International Airport) [GRU Airport People Mover]. After years of delays, the line opened to **airport employees on 3 December 2025**, then to the **general public on 20 February 2026** [GRU Airport People Mover].

case.question: Which 2026 metro line in Paris, France opened on February 14, 2026 with a main span of 39.6 km?

agent_run.question: Which 2026 metro line in Paris, France opened on February 14, 2026 with a main span of 39.6 km?
agent_run.answer:
The question contains a **false premise** — actually two of them. Based on my research in Wikipedia, here is what the sources actually say:

1. **No Paris metro line opened on February 14, 2026.** According to Wikipedia, none of the new Grand Paris Express lines (15, 16, 17, or 18) are recorded as having opened on that date. Line 15 South is projected for **April 2027**, Line 16 is planned for **mid-2027 to 2028**, Line 17 for **2027–2030**, and Line 18's first phase is scheduled for **October 1, 2026** [Grand Paris Express][Paris Metro Line 15][Paris Metro Line 16].

2. **"Main span" is not a metric used for metro lines.** "Main span" is a term used for bridges (referring to the longest span between two supports), not for rail transit lines. Metro lines are measured in length (kilometres), not by main span. No Paris metro line has a "main span of 39.6 km" — this concept does not apply [Grand Paris Express].

The question appears to conflate characteristics of a bridge (main span) with those of a metro line, and the specific date and measurement given do not correspond to any real Paris metro opening described in Wikipedia.

- Paris Metro Line 16

You're describing the **Camp Hill Line** (also known as the Camp Hill chord or Camp Hill railway line) in Birmingham.

This line runs between **Kings Norton** and **Birmingham New Street** (the city's primary central station), passing through areas including Moseley and Camp Hill. It was historically used primarily by freight traffic and some longer-distance passenger services for decades after regular local passenger services ceased, and plans have been in place to reopen it to regular passenger services.

The ELR (Engineer's Line Reference) designation you mention — covering the St Andrews Junction to Kings Norton line — corresponds to this route, and the reopening (to passenger services) has been associated with the **2026** timeframe as part of wider West Midlands rail improvement plans.

## Answer: **Line 5 Eglinton (Eglinton Crosstown LRT), Toronto — 25 stations**

Among all 2026-opened metro, monorail, tramway, LRT, and commuter rail lines documented on Wikipedia, **Line 5 Eglinton** in Toronto holds the highest station count, with **25 stations** along its 19-kilometre route [Line 5 Eglinton].

- Service began on **8 February 2026** as a phased opening, operated by the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) [Line 5 Eglinton][2026 in rail transport].
- It is a **light rail transit (LRT)** line running mostly underground in its central section along Eglinton Avenue, from Mount Dennis to Kennedy station [Line 5 Eglinton].

### Tie-breaker — Gauge
The question's tie-breaker is directly satisfied: the Wikipedia article for Line 5 Eglinton explicitly states that *"It uses standard gauge rather than the broad Toronto gauge used by the three older heavy rail subway lines."* [Line 5 Eglinton]. That is, while Toronto's Lines 1, 2, and 4 (the older heavy-rail subways) use a broader, non-standard gauge, Line 5 Eglinton is built to **standard gauge (1,435 mm)** — making it the standout match even if a tie-break were needed.

case.question: Among the 2026-opened railway / transit lines documented on Wikipedia in this set, identify the line whose construction began earliest and the line whose construction began latest, and state both construction-start dates. Tie-breaker: only count lines whose Wikipedia article gives an explicit construction-start date.
case.expected_behavior: Earliest: Line 5 Eglinton — first phase construction began in 2011 (specifically tunnelling commenced after July 2010 TBM order; construction officially in 2011). Latest: Line 6 Ningbo Rail Transit — construction started December 28, 2022. Other documented dates: Jinhua–Jiande HSR officially began January 7, 2020; Line S2 (Ma'anshan section) started December 30, 2020 (Nanjing section December 28, 2021). Line 5 Eglinton is the clear earliest; Line 6 Ningbo is the clear latest. The agent must compare construction-start dates across articles.
